intent: Parser-stress: a -C field-comparison rule (-C uid!=euid, the privilege-transition idiom) plus a 10-field AND'ed syscall rule near the 64-field limit. Both valid auditctl grammar (f3 section 2.3-2.4). Catches a parser that chokes on -C or on long -F chains.

rules.note: execve narrowed by a uid!=euid field-comparison (privilege transition). -C maps to AUDIT_COMPARE_UID_TO_EUID (libaudit.c:1158). Narrowed => MEDIUM (~0.3x unrestricted execve, f3 section 6).
openat narrowed by 9 AND'ed -F fields (auid>=1000 excludes daemon noise). Heavily narrowed => low end of MEDIUM. 10 fields total, well under the 64-field limit (auditctl(8)).

suspected_trap: A naive parser that does not handle the -C field-comparison flag (distinct from -F; -C takes two field NAMES and a comparator, mapping to AUDIT_COMPARE_* per libaudit.c:1137-1180) will fail to parse rule A, or mis-tier it. A parser with a low -F field cap will reject rule B's 10-field chain even though auditctl allows up to 64. Both rules are MEDIUM additive (narrowed); a model that ignores the narrowing and tiers the bare -S execve / -S openat as HIGH over-warns.
